misconceptionSummary: Students regroup numbers without checking whether the operation allows it.

lines.text: Swap to make a round number appear

steps.narration: The commutative law lets you swap the order of addition. Spotting that eighty-three and seventeen sum to one hundred is much easier if you reorder them — seventeen plus eighty-three becomes obvious.
The associative law lets you regroup which numbers you add first. Four and six pair to make ten — then adding seventeen is trivial. Choosing the right grouping makes mental arithmetic much faster.
Combine both laws: reorder using commutativity, then regroup using associativity. Pairing twenty-three with seven and forty-five with fifty-five reveals two round-number totals that add instantly.

visuals.label: Reorder → regroup → spot pairs that make round numbers

steps.narration: One hundred and thirty. Two simple laws — commutativity and associativity — turned a four-number sum into two trivial additions.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: adding left to right without looking for friendly pairs. Use commutativity to reorder and associativity to regroup — find pairs that sum to 10, 100, or other round numbers.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Before you add a long list, scan for pairs that make round numbers. Reorder and regroup freely — addition allows both.
